hampta-pass-trek.title: Manali Drive to Jobra and Acclimatisation Walk to Chikka
hampta-pass-trek.trailType: birch forest path, riverside trail along the Rani Nallah
hampta-pass-trek.description: The Hampta crossing begins with a bumpy but scenic drive out of Manali, the road climbing past Prini and the apple orchards of Sethan before the tarmac finally gives out at Jobra, a scatter of Gaddi shepherd huts that marks the true trailhead. From here the group shoulders daypacks and drops onto a soft forest path running parallel to the Rani Nallah, its glacial water churning white over grey boulders just below the trail. Birch and maple crowd close on both sides, their leaves rustling in a wind that already carries a noticeably sharper edge than the valley floor, and the first hour is as much about settling into rhythm as covering ground. Wild roses and clumps of nettle line the damper stretches near the stream, while higher up the corridor opens briefly onto boulder fields where the group pauses to look back at the receding green folds of the Kullu valley. Chikka, the day's destination, is a broad grassy shelf pinched between the rushing Rani Nallah and a steep forested slope, used for generations as a shepherd halt before the climb toward Balu ka Ghera begins in earnest. Tents go up on flattened ground near the water's edge, and the short distance covered today is entirely deliberate, giving bodies a gentle first taste of altitude before the real gain begins tomorrow. Guides use the afternoon to check gear, redistribute loads if needed, and walk through the week's plan in detail, including how weather on Hampta's exposed pass can shift within hours. As dusk falls, the temperature drops fast enough that down jackets appear at the fire circle, and the sound of the nallah becomes a constant backdrop to a dinner eaten under a sky already noticeably clearer and starrier than in Manali. It is a short day by distance, but an essential one for settling nerves and legs alike before Hampta's more demanding stretches begin.
highlights: Drive from Manali through Sethan's orchard belt
First walk beside the glacial Rani Nallah
Gentle acclimatisation pacing on day one
Shepherd halt campsite at Chikka
tips: Keep the first day's pace deliberately slow regardless of how fresh you feel
Carry a rain shell even in clear weather, as this corridor can turn misty fast
Fill water bottles at the nallah before pitching camp, since flow is cleanest upstream of the site

hampta-pass-trek.title: Chikka to Balu ka Ghera through Alpine Meadow
hampta-pass-trek.trailType: boulder-strewn riverbank, moraine slope, open alpine meadow
hampta-pass-trek.description: Leaving Chikka, the trail immediately demands more attention, weaving across a boulder field where the Rani Nallah has scattered rock debris over centuries, and trekkers pick a careful line from stone to stone with poles doing much of the balancing work. Beyond the boulders, the path climbs onto steadily narrowing moraine slopes, the vegetation thinning from birch scrub to low juniper and finally to bare alpine turf as the valley walls close in on either side. Waterfalls drop from hanging side valleys at intervals, some crossed on makeshift log bridges and others requiring a direct wade through icy, ankle-deep water that jolts everyone fully awake regardless of the hour. As the gradient eases in the afternoon, the valley opens into the wide, undulating meadow known as Balu ka Ghera, ringed by rock walls and fed by multiple snowmelt streams that braid across the grass in silver threads. The name, loosely translating to a sandy enclosure, reflects the pale, granular soil visible in patches between tufts of grass, distinct from the darker earth seen lower down the valley. This is one of the most photographed campsites on the entire route, with views back down the valley toward Chikka on one side and, ahead, a first glimpse of the rocky notch that marks Hampta Pass itself. Marmots are frequently spotted darting between rocks near camp, and the cold at this altitude arrives early, well before actual sunset. Guides use the extended evening light to point out the pass's exact line on the ridge above and to brief the group thoroughly on tomorrow's demanding crossing, including pacing strategy and what to expect from the steep final climb. Dinner is eaten quickly tonight, appetite often blunted slightly by the altitude gain, and most trekkers retreat to their tents earlier than the previous night, aware that an early start is essential for the pass crossing ahead.
highlights: Boulder-hopping along the Rani Nallah
Multiple glacial stream and waterfall crossings
Wide open meadow camp at Balu ka Ghera
First clear sighting of the Hampta Pass notch
tips: Waterproof your boots or carry sandals for the colder stream crossings
Eat something even if appetite dips, tomorrow is the toughest day of the trek
Zip tents fully at night, as wind at this campsite picks up noticeably after dark

hampta-pass-trek.title: Balu ka Ghera over Hampta Pass to Shea Goru
hampta-pass-trek.trailType: steep scree ascent, glacier-edged pass, boulder descent into Lahaul
hampta-pass-trek.description: This is the day the entire trek pivots around, and the group departs Balu ka Ghera in cold, early light to make the most of firm ground before the sun softens any remaining snow patches on the ascent. The climb toward the pass steepens quickly, following a rough line of scree and loose rock that zigzags up the headwall, with occasional stretches of old, hardened snow that require careful, deliberate footing. Breathing gets noticeably harder as the group gains the final few hundred metres, and short rest stops become more frequent, though the sense of anticipation keeps most legs moving despite the thinning air. Cresting Hampta Pass at just over 14,000 feet delivers one of the most abrupt landscape transitions in the Indian Himalaya: behind lies the green, moisture-fed Kullu valley, while ahead the Lahaul side unfolds in shades of brown, grey, and ochre, utterly stripped of the vegetation seen just metres away on the other side of the ridge. Prayer flags strung across a cairn mark the pass itself, and on a clear day the view extends toward the rugged Spiti-facing ranges and down into the valley the group will descend through for the remainder of the trek. The way down is steep and demands full concentration, dropping through loose scree and boulder fields where trekking poles prove essential, the terrain growing progressively drier and starker with every switchback lost. Shea Goru, the night's camp, sits in a stark, wide valley bed beside a milky glacial stream, entirely without the greenery of the Kullu side, its silence broken mostly by wind moving across bare rock. Reaching camp after a genuinely long day brings real relief, and dinner conversation is dominated by replays of the crossing itself, each trekker comparing notes on the exact moment the landscape flipped from meadow to desert underfoot.
highlights: Crossing Hampta Pass at approximately 14,100 ft
Dramatic shift from Kullu greenery to Lahaul's arid terrain
Steep scree descent into the Chandra valley catchment
Stark glacial camp at Shea Goru
tips: Start in the dark to reach the pass before the snow softens
Sunglasses are essential, as glare off any remaining snow can be intense
Pace the descent carefully, loose scree causes more falls than the climb itself

hampta-pass-trek.title: Shea Goru Descent to Chatru
hampta-pass-trek.trailType: arid riverbed track, sandy moraine, Lahaul valley trail
hampta-pass-trek.description: With the pass now behind the group, today's walk trades yesterday's tension for a long, steady descent through a landscape that feels almost lunar in its bareness, the Chandra valley's tributary stream carving a pale channel through slopes of sand, scree, and wind-sculpted rock. The trail follows this stream loosely, crossing it a few times on stepping stones as the valley widens, and the utter absence of trees or continuous grass cover here stands in sharp contrast to every previous day's greenery. Wind is a near-constant companion through this stretch, funnelled down the open valley with enough force that buffs and hoods stay on for most of the walk, and conversation tends to fade into a steady, meditative rhythm of footsteps instead. Small clusters of blue poppy and other hardy alpine flowers occasionally punctuate the otherwise stark ground in the height of summer, a surprising splash of colour against the browns and greys dominating the scenery. As the trail loses height, the valley broadens further and the first signs of the Manali-Kaza highway traffic become visible in the far distance, a strange, welcome reminder of the wider world after two days of comparative wilderness. Chatru itself is a bare, windswept flat beside the Chandra river, historically used as a halting point by road workers and now a favourite overnight stop for trekkers completing the Hampta crossing, often shared with groups doing the reverse route or overlanding bikers on the Manali-Leh road. The Chandra river here runs a distinct milky turquoise, fed by glaciers upstream, and camp is pitched a safe distance back from its banks. Evening temperatures drop sharply once the sun dips behind the ridge, and the exposed, open character of this camp makes for a very different night's sleep compared to the sheltered meadows on the Kullu side of the pass.
highlights: Long descent through the arid Chandra valley catchment
Wildflowers surviving in an otherwise stark, dry landscape
First distant views of the Manali-Kaza highway
Windswept riverside camp at Chatru beside the milky Chandra river
tips: Keep a windproof jacket handy all day, this valley is rarely still
Watch your footing on the sandy moraine sections, which shift underfoot
Secure loose items in your tent overnight, as gusts here can be strong

hampta-pass-trek.title: Chatru to Chandratal (Seasonal) and Drive Back to Manali
hampta-pass-trek.trailType: motorable mountain road, gravel lakeside path
hampta-pass-trek.description: The final day shifts gears entirely, trading trekking boots for a long but spectacular drive, though when seasonal road conditions allow, most groups build in a stop at Chandratal, the crescent-shaped lake that has become almost synonymous with this region of Himachal. The road out of Chatru climbs steadily through more of Lahaul's stark, folded terrain before a short side track branches off toward the lake, and the first sight of Chandratal's improbably still, blue-green water set against a bowl of bare mountains rarely fails to draw an audible reaction from the group. A gentle walk around a section of the lake's grassy fore-shore reveals its full, elongated crescent shape, the feature that gives it the name Moon Lake, and on calm days the surrounding peaks reflect on the water with striking clarity. Local legend holds that fairies visit the lake at night to dance on its shores, a story shepherds and traders have told for generations along this old trade route between Lahaul and Spiti. After time spent at the lake, weather and road conditions permitting, the group re-boards vehicles for the long drive back over Rohtang Pass toward Manali, the route retracing much of the historic trade corridor that once linked Kullu with Ladakh via Lahaul. The drive itself is scenic in its own right, passing glacier tongues, roadside waterfalls, and the dramatic hairpins below Rohtang before descending back into the familiar green of the Kullu valley by evening. Arriving in Manali marks the formal end of the trek, and the drive gives everyone a final, unhurried chance to look back at the pass crossed two days earlier, now a distant notch on the skyline, and to reflect on a route that manages to compress an astonishing range of Himalayan landscapes into just five days.
highlights: Seasonal visit to the crescent-shaped Chandratal lake
Local fairy-dance legend associated with Moon Lake
Scenic drive back over Rohtang Pass
Trek conclusion in Manali by evening
tips: Chandratal access depends entirely on road and weather conditions, so keep expectations flexible
Carry warm layers for the drive, as Rohtang stays cold even in peak summer
Keep travel documents handy, as this stretch sometimes has checkpoints

bhrigu-lake-trek.title: Manali to Gulaba and Trek to Rola Kholi Camp
bhrigu-lake-trek.trailType: pine forest track, open pasture, gentle ridge climb
bhrigu-lake-trek.description: The Bhrigu Lake trek starts with a short drive from Manali along the Rohtang road to Gulaba, a checkpoint and grazing ground just beyond the tree line where the road begins its climb toward the pass proper. From the roadside, the trail cuts away from the highway almost immediately, entering a stand of chir pine before opening onto rolling pasture dotted with grazing sheep and the temporary huts of Gaddi shepherd families who spend the summer here with their flocks. The gradient is forgiving by Himalayan standards, climbing in long, easy stretches rather than sharp switchbacks, which makes this an appealing route for trekkers newer to high-altitude walking. As height is gained, views open steadily back toward the Rohtang road snaking up the opposite slope, and the Beas valley recedes into a green corridor far below. Wildflowers, particularly potentilla and clusters of wild geranium, colour the meadow edges through the summer months, and marmots occasionally break cover near rockier patches close to camp. Rola Kholi itself is a grassy shelf tucked below a low ridge, chosen for its access to a reliable spring and its sheltered position relative to the wind that picks up higher on the route toward Bhrigu. Tents are pitched with clear views toward the Pir Panjal's snow-streaked crest, and the relatively gentle effort required to reach this first camp leaves most trekkers with energy to spare for an evening stroll along the ridge above the site. Guides use the stop to explain the mythology tied to Bhrigu Lake, said to be named for the sage Bhrigu who is believed to have meditated at its shores, and to outline the following day's longer, higher push toward the lake itself. Dinner is taken early, the mountain air turning sharply cold once the sun dips behind the western ridge, and the first true taste of alpine cold sets the tone for the days ahead.
highlights: Gentle ridge walk from the Gulaba roadhead
Gaddi shepherd pastures and grazing meadows
Early views of the Pir Panjal's snow crest
Sheltered first camp at Rola Kholi with a reliable spring
tips: This is an easier first day, use it to test your pace rather than rushing
Carry a personal water bottle refill, the Rola Kholi spring is the last reliable source before higher camps
Sunscreen matters even on overcast days at this altitude

bhrigu-lake-trek.title: Rola Kholi to Bhrigu Lake and Back to Camp
bhrigu-lake-trek.trailType: open ridge climb, alpine meadow, high-altitude lake basin
bhrigu-lake-trek.description: Today's climb leaves the last low scrub behind within the first hour, the trail rising steadily along an increasingly bare ridge that offers uninterrupted views in almost every direction as height accumulates. The going is a mix of grassy slope and looser rocky ground, and the thinning air makes the final approach noticeably more tiring than the distance alone would suggest, with short pauses becoming more frequent as the group nears 14,000 feet. Bhrigu Lake itself appears with little warning, a still, glacial pool set in a wide, otherwise treeless bowl below the ridge, its water taking on an almost metallic grey-blue sheen under overcast skies and a brighter turquoise when the sun breaks through. According to local belief, the lake never fully freezes even through the harshest winters, an oddity attributed to its association with the sage Bhrigu, and its waters are considered sacred by communities across the Kullu valley, some of whom make an annual pilgrimage here during the Fagli festival. The setting is starkly beautiful, ringed by rounded, grass-covered peaks rather than jagged rock, giving the basin an open, exposed feel very different from more enclosed glacial lakes elsewhere in Himachal. Trekkers typically spend a good half hour at the water's edge, and on genuinely clear days the panorama extends toward the Dhauladhar range to the south and the deeper folds of the Kullu valley to the west. The return to Rola Kholi retraces the same ridge, considerably quicker downhill though still demanding careful footing on the looser sections, and reaching camp again by mid-afternoon leaves time to rest and dry out any gear before the evening chill sets in. Dinner conversation tends to circle back repeatedly to the lake's changing colours and the strange, slightly eerie stillness of its water.
highlights: Reaching Bhrigu Lake at roughly 14,100 ft
The lake's unusual year-round unfrozen water
Wide, open grass-ridge terrain uncommon on other Himachal treks
Panoramic views toward the Dhauladhar range
tips: Start early, as afternoon cloud cover often rolls in and obscures the lake by midday
Wear layers you can shed quickly, temperature swings fast between exertion and rest stops
Do not disturb offerings left at the lake's edge by local pilgrims

bhrigu-lake-trek.title: Rola Kholi to Khanul Camp via the Ridge Traverse
bhrigu-lake-trek.trailType: grassy ridge traverse, scattered boulder field, descending pasture
bhrigu-lake-trek.description: Rather than retracing the exact outbound route on the final approach toward Vashisht, the trek shifts today onto a parallel ridge line, traversing a stretch of high pasture that most standard out-and-back itineraries skip entirely. The walking is a gentler blend of grass and scattered boulder, with the trail hugging the contour rather than climbing hard, and the views compensate generously for the modest technical demands, taking in a wide sweep of the Beas valley on one side and glimpses of yesterday's lake basin on the other. Shepherd cairns mark the route at intervals, a practical navigation aid for Gaddi herders moving flocks between grazing grounds through the short summer season, and their significance is often explained by guides familiar with the seasonal migration patterns still practised in this part of Kullu. As the trail begins to lose height in the afternoon, birch and juniper scrub reappear for the first time since Rola Kholi, softening the stark alpine character of the previous two days and signalling the gradual return toward the treeline. Khanul camp sits on a broad, sheltered pasture below this returning vegetation belt, chosen for its position roughly midway along the descent toward Vashisht and for the small stream that runs along its lower edge. The site offers one of the trek's better sunset vantage points, with the Pir Panjal's western ridges catching the day's last light in shifting shades of pink and gold. Evenings here tend to be more relaxed than the previous two nights, altitude-related fatigue easing noticeably now that the group has dropped below 13,000 feet, and conversation often turns toward comparing notes on the trek's varied terrain, from Gulaba's gentle pastures to the stark grandeur of Bhrigu itself. A final briefing on the next day's descent into Vashisht closes out the evening around the fire.
highlights: Ridge traverse skipping the standard return route
Shepherd cairns marking traditional grazing paths
Return of birch and juniper scrub on the lower slopes
Sunset views over the Pir Panjal's western ridges from Khanul
tips: Use trekking poles on the boulder sections, footing here is less even than it looks
This camp tends to be milder than Rola Kholi, but still layer up after sunset
Charge devices fully tonight if relying on solar power banks, tomorrow is the exit day

bhrigu-lake-trek.title: Khanul Descent to Vashisht and Trek Conclusion
bhrigu-lake-trek.trailType: forested descent, terraced village fields, stone village lanes
bhrigu-lake-trek.description: The last day is almost entirely downhill, and it delivers one of the more dramatic vegetation changes on the whole route, the trail dropping from Khanul's open pasture into thickening birch, then oak, and finally deodar forest as the village of Vashisht draws nearer with every switchback. The path is well trodden by local herders and villagers, and stone-lined sections appear increasingly often the lower the trail descends, hinting at the settled, terraced farmland waiting below. Apple orchards begin to punctuate the forest edge in the last hour or two, their neat rows a clear sign of returning civilisation after three days of comparative wilderness, and the sound of the Beas river, distant but growing steadily louder, becomes a constant undertone. Vashisht itself is a village with genuine antiquity, its stone Rama and Shiva temples dating back centuries and its natural hot sulphur springs drawing visitors from well beyond Himachal, and there is a particular satisfaction in easing tired legs into the warm communal baths shortly after finishing the descent. Narrow lanes lined with small shops selling shawls, tea, and mountain snacks give the village a lived-in, slightly bohemian character quite different from the pastoral solitude of the previous days. Guides typically use the walk's final stretch to recap the trek's arc, from Gulaba's gentle meadows to Bhrigu's sacred, unfrozen lake and the quieter ridge traverse that followed, framing Vashisht's warm springs as a fitting, well-earned close. From here it is a short onward journey into Manali proper, and the trek formally concludes with that easy, contented tiredness that comes from four days spent almost entirely above the noise of ordinary life.
highlights: Dramatic forest transition from birch to deodar on the descent
Vashisht's ancient stone temples
Natural hot sulphur springs at trek's end
Apple orchards signalling the return to valley life
tips: Save time and energy for the Vashisht hot springs, they are the perfect end to this trek
Keep small change handy for the village's tea stalls and shops
Confirm your Manali transfer or accommodation before starting the final descent

beas-kund-trek.title: Manali via Dhundi to Bakarthach Camp
beas-kund-trek.trailType: riverside gravel track, birch grove, open moraine flat
beas-kund-trek.description: The short drive from Manali to Dhundi runs along the Solang road past ski lifts and paragliding launch points before the tarmac ends at a scatter of tea shacks marking the trailhead. From Dhundi, the trail follows the young Beas river upstream, its water here still narrow and fast, tumbling over rounded stones in a corridor lined with birch and the occasional stand of willow. The path is gentle underfoot, a wide, well-used track shaped by decades of shepherd and mule traffic moving supplies toward the higher grazing grounds, and progress feels easy even for those trekking at altitude for the first time. Snow bridges occasionally span the river in early season, a reminder of how much winter accumulation still lingers this close to the Rohtang corridor even as summer settles over the lower valley. As the trail gains height, the birch thins into scattered juniper and finally bare moraine, and the valley widens into a broad, stony flat where the river braids into several channels across gravel bars. Bakarthach, the day's camp, takes its name from the Gaddi shepherds who have long grazed goats here through the summer months, and their temporary stone shelters are still visible scattered across the flat. Views from camp stretch up-valley toward the rockier terrain that tomorrow's walk to Beas Kund will cross, while behind, the trail back down toward Dhundi and the Solang valley remains visible for much of the afternoon. Being one of the more accessible high-altitude camps near Manali, Bakarthach can see a fair number of day-trekkers passing through, lending the site a sociable atmosphere in contrast to more remote camps elsewhere in the region. Dinner is eaten as the last light fades from the surrounding ridgelines, and the guide outlines the following day's walk to the lake and back.
highlights: Gentle riverside walk beside the infant Beas river
Early-season snow bridges near Dhundi
Gaddi shepherd grazing grounds at Bakarthach
Wide moraine flat camp with valley views
tips: This route sees plenty of day visitors, start early to enjoy quieter trail sections
Watch your footing on the gravel bars near the river, stones shift underfoot
Keep an extra layer accessible, wind picks up on this open flat by late afternoon

beas-kund-trek.title: Bakarthach to Beas Kund and Back to Camp
beas-kund-trek.trailType: boulder and moraine trail, glacial approach, alpine lake basin
beas-kund-trek.description: The walk to Beas Kund climbs through increasingly rugged terrain, the trail picking a line across glacial moraine and scattered boulder fields left behind by the retreating ice that once filled this entire upper valley. Underfoot, loose rock and sand alternate with firmer stretches of packed scree, and the pace naturally slows as the group gains altitude, the thinning air making even moderate gradients feel more demanding than they would lower down. The valley narrows as it climbs, framed by the steep faces of Hanuman Tibba and Friendship Peak, both popular objectives for mountaineering expeditions based out of Manali, and their presence lends the walk a distinctly mountaineering atmosphere rather than a purely pastoral one. Beas Kund itself is a modest but striking glacial lake tucked directly beneath these peaks, considered by Hindu tradition to be the source of the Beas river and named for the sage Vyasa, who is said to have meditated at this very spot. The water sits an intense, cold blue-green, fed directly by snowmelt and small hanging glaciers on the surrounding cliffs, and chunks of old ice occasionally float near its shaded edges even in high summer. Trekkers typically spend a generous stretch of time here, absorbing the scale of the amphitheatre of rock and ice that encircles the lake, before beginning the return journey along the same route. The descent moves considerably faster than the climb, though loose scree sections still demand attention, and reaching Bakarthach again by mid-afternoon leaves ample time to rest before dinner. This out-and-back structure, distinct from routes that continue onward past the lake, gives Beas Kund its particular character as an intense but compact high-altitude objective completed in a single demanding day from camp.
highlights: Reaching the sacred, glacier-fed Beas Kund at over 12,000 ft
Close views of Hanuman Tibba and Friendship Peak
Mythological association with the sage Vyasa and the Beas river's source
Old glacial ice visible near the lake's shaded margins
tips: This is a long, technical day for a two-camp trek, pace yourself on the moraine sections
Sunglasses and sun protection are essential given the exposed, reflective terrain
Carry extra snacks, appetite often dips at this altitude but energy needs stay high

beas-kund-trek.title: Bakarthach to Dhundi and Drive to Manali
beas-kund-trek.trailType: riverside gravel track, birch grove descent, roadhead trail
beas-kund-trek.description: The final morning retraces yesterday's outbound path back down toward Dhundi, though the walk feels markedly different in reverse, with the Beas river now on the opposite side and views opening toward the Solang valley's ski slopes rather than the peaks framing Beas Kund. Descending is quick and easy after two days of harder climbing, the birch groves near the lower stretch offering welcome shade as the morning sun strengthens, and the sound of the river grows steadily louder as the trail loses height. Marmots and occasional Himalayan pika dart between rocks near the trail's edge, more visible now that the group moves at a relaxed pace rather than the concentrated effort of the previous day's lake approach. Dhundi appears with its familiar cluster of tea shacks well before midday, and the transition from mountain quiet to roadside bustle, with taxis, day-trip groups, and the occasional grazing pony, feels abrupt after two nights spent almost entirely alone on the moraine flats above. From Dhundi, a short drive winds back down through Solang, past its cable car towers and zip-line rigs that stand oddly at odds with the wilder terrain just an hour's walk away, before rejoining the main road into Manali. Guides often use this drive to answer final questions about the peaks and features seen from Beas Kund, and to point out landmarks along the Solang valley that connect to other trekking and mountaineering routes in the area. Arriving back in Manali by early afternoon brings the trek to a close, its three days having packed in a genuinely dramatic arc from riverside forest to a sacred, glacier-rimmed lake and back, all within easy striking distance of the town itself.
highlights: Quick, easy descent through birch groves back to Dhundi
Marmot and pika sightings along the lower trail
Contrast between Solang's tourist infrastructure and the wild upper valley
Trek conclusion with an early afternoon return to Manali
tips: Keep your camera ready on the descent, wildlife sightings are more frequent at this relaxed pace
Confirm your Manali drop-off point with the operator before setting off
Grab lunch at Solang if time allows, it is one of the last easy food stops before town

patalsu-peak-trek.title: Solang to Patalsu Base Camp
patalsu-peak-trek.trailType: pine forest switchbacks, open ridge meadow, rocky approach slope
patalsu-peak-trek.description: The climb toward Patalsu begins right from the edge of Solang's ski slopes, the trail cutting away from the paragliding fields and rope-tow towers into a quieter forest of pine and horse chestnut that quickly muffles the noise of the valley below. Switchbacks climb steadily through this belt, the gradient firm but manageable, and gaps in the canopy offer intermittent views back over Solang's broad meadow, easily recognisable by the cluster of cafes and adventure-sport rigs dotting its floor. As the forest thins, the trail breaks onto an open ridge shoulder where grass replaces tree cover almost entirely, and the walking becomes noticeably more exposed to both sun and wind, with views expanding rapidly in every direction. This ridge line is used seasonally by shepherds moving flocks up from the Solang side, and stone livestock shelters mark the route at intervals, offering a rough gauge of progress even without a map. Patalsu Base Camp sits on a grassy shelf just below the peak's final rocky approach, chosen for its flat ground and relative shelter from the wind that sweeps more forcefully across the exposed summit ridge above. From camp, the views are already substantial, taking in the Solang valley's full length and a first close look at the folded ridgelines separating Kullu from the Lahaul side of the range. The final stretch of trail up to the summit, visible from camp as a rocky spine above the meadow, is pointed out in detail during the evening briefing, along with realistic timing expectations for tomorrow's early start. Temperatures fall quickly once the sun drops behind the ridge, and most trekkers retreat to their tents soon after dinner, keen to bank as much rest as possible before the demanding summit push ahead.
highlights: Forest switchbacks rising directly from Solang's ski slopes
Open ridge meadow with expanding valley views
Shepherd shelters marking the seasonal grazing route
Base camp views toward the Lahaul-facing ridgelines
tips: Break in your boots before this trek, the summit day involves sustained rocky ground
Fill water bottles at the last reliable stream before the ridge opens up
Sleep early tonight, tomorrow's summit push starts well before sunrise

patalsu-peak-trek.title: Patalsu Peak Summit and Descent to Solang
patalsu-peak-trek.trailType: rocky summit ridge, loose scree, long forested descent
patalsu-peak-trek.description: The summit push begins in darkness, headlamps picking out the trail as it climbs away from camp along an increasingly rocky spine, the gradient steepening noticeably as the final stretch to Patalsu's top comes into view against a lightening sky. Loose rock and scree make careful footing essential through this section, and the thinning air slows pace even on relatively short pitches, the last few hundred feet feeling considerably more strenuous than the map distance suggests. Reaching the summit at just under 14,000 feet rewards the effort with one of the most complete panoramas available on a short trek from Manali: the Solang and Kullu valleys spread out to the south, the Pir Panjal's jagged crest running along the northern horizon, and on exceptionally clear mornings, distant glimpses of peaks toward the Spiti and Lahaul border. Many trekkers linger here longer than planned, reluctant to leave a viewpoint that took real effort to reach, though guides keep a close watch on the time given how long the subsequent descent will take. The way down initially retraces the ascent route back to base camp, but from there the trail diverges, dropping through the ridge meadows and then back into the pine forest via a slightly different line that eventually rejoins the Solang valley floor near its cluster of cafes and adventure-sport operators. This descent is long, covering nearly five and a half thousand feet of elevation loss in a single push, and legs already tired from the early summit effort are tested hard on the lower switchbacks. Arriving back in Solang by afternoon, amid paragliders circling overhead and the general bustle of the valley's tourist infrastructure, provides a strangely fitting, almost jarring contrast to the solitude of the summit just hours earlier, and marks a satisfying, if leg-weary, conclusion to a short but genuinely demanding two-day climb.
highlights: Summiting Patalsu Peak at nearly 14,000 ft
Panoramic views spanning the Pir Panjal and distant Spiti-facing ranges
Long, varied descent through ridge meadow and pine forest
Return to Solang's lively valley floor by afternoon
tips: Start the summit push well before dawn to summit before winds and cloud build up
Trekking poles significantly ease the long descent on tired knees
Carry more water than you think you need, there are no reliable sources above base camp

deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.title: Manali to Jagatsukh and Trek to Jobri
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.trailType: deodar forest trail, terraced village fields, riverside path along the Duhangan
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.description: The approach to Deo Tibba begins in Jagatsukh, a village of genuine historical weight in the Kullu valley, once its ancient capital and still home to a pair of weathered stone temples dedicated to Shiva and the Sandhya Gayatri deity, their carvings softened by centuries of monsoon rain. From the village, the trail leaves the terraced barley fields behind and enters a hushed deodar forest, following the Duhangan stream as it tumbles down from the glaciers feeding Deo Tibba far above. The climb is steady rather than steep, and the forest canopy keeps the walk pleasantly shaded even as the sun climbs higher, with occasional clearings offering glimpses back down toward Jagatsukh's fields and the wider Kullu valley beyond. Wild strawberries and ferns crowd the trail's damper margins in season, and the sound of the Duhangan, never far from the path, provides a constant, cooling backdrop to the walk. As the forest opens into a broader valley shelf, Jobri appears, a modest camping ground used by shepherds and trekking groups alike, chosen for its flat ground and proximity to reliable water. Views from camp already hint at the scale of what lies ahead, with the lower flanks of the Deo Tibba massif visible through gaps in the surrounding ridgeline. This first day is intentionally moderate, allowing bodies to adjust gradually to altitude before the terrain grows more demanding over the coming days, and the relatively short distance leaves time in the afternoon to rest, explore the immediate area, or simply watch the light shift across the forested slopes. Dinner is taken early against the backdrop of a stream running loud in the near-dark, and the guide outlines the days ahead, emphasising the steady, cumulative nature of this particular route toward one of the Kullu valley's most striking peaks.
highlights: Ancient temples of Jagatsukh, the historic Kullu capital
Shaded deodar forest walk beside the Duhangan stream
Gentle first-day acclimatisation pacing
Early glimpses of the Deo Tibba massif from camp
tips: Visit Jagatsukh's temples before setting off if time allows, they are easily missed once trekking begins
Wild strawberries along the trail are safe to sample in season, but forage sparingly
Keep this day's pace unhurried, the trek's demands increase steadily from here

deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.title: Jobri to Seri Meadow
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.trailType: birch and rhododendron forest, riverside meadow trail
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.description: Beyond Jobri, the deodar forest gradually gives way to birch and increasingly dense rhododendron, the trail climbing in a series of manageable stages alongside the Duhangan, whose water grows colder and more heavily silted with glacial sediment the higher the trail goes. Rhododendron blooms spectacularly here through late spring, painting entire hillsides in shades of red and pink, though even outside that window the gnarled, moss-draped branches lend the forest a distinctly old, slightly mysterious character. Several stream crossings punctuate the day, mostly on stable log bridges though a couple require a careful direct wade, and the cold, clear water offers a welcome moment of relief on warmer afternoons. As the trail gains height, the forest begins to thin into scattered stands of birch interspersed with open grass, and the first proper views of Deo Tibba's glaciated upper slopes appear through breaks in the canopy, considerably closer and more imposing than the distant glimpses from Jobri. Seri, the day's destination, is a wide, gently sloping meadow favoured by shepherd communities for summer grazing, its grass kept short and springy by generations of livestock, and the camp here enjoys one of the more open, unobstructed outlooks on the entire route. Marmots are commonly seen darting between rocks at the meadow's edges, and the relative gentleness of the terrain compared to what lies ahead makes this an easy, restorative stop despite the steady altitude gain achieved over the day. Evening light on the peaks surrounding Seri often turns a deep gold before fading to blue, and the guide uses the calm atmosphere to talk through the trek's remaining stages, including the notably steeper ground that begins tomorrow as the route approaches Tainta and the base of the Deo Tibba glacier system itself.
highlights: Rhododendron and birch forest corridor along the Duhangan
Multiple stream crossings with increasingly glacial water
First close views of Deo Tibba's upper glaciated slopes
Wide, open grazing meadow camp at Seri
tips: Cross streams carefully on wet log bridges, they are more slippery than they look
Layer up for the evening, Seri's open exposure means a sharper temperature drop after sunset
Keep cameras handy through this stretch, the rhododendron corridor is one of the trek's highlights

deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.title: Seri to Tainta Camp
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.trailType: moraine-edge trail, boulder field, high alpine pasture
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.description: Leaving Seri's soft grazing meadow behind, the trail today grows noticeably rockier, tracing the edge of old moraine deposits left by the Duhangan glacier's historic retreat and crossing occasional boulder fields where careful footing becomes essential. Vegetation thins with every few hundred metres gained, birch giving way to scattered juniper and finally sparse alpine grass clinging to increasingly bare, rocky ground. The Duhangan itself, now reduced to a smaller but more turbulent glacial stream, stays close to the route for much of the morning, its banks lined with loose scree that shifts underfoot in places. As the trail climbs further, views expand dramatically toward the jagged rock spires and ice fields crowning Deo Tibba and its neighbouring peaks, the scale of the terrain ahead becoming genuinely apparent for the first time on the trek. Tainta, the day's camp, sits on a relatively flat pasture wedged between moraine ridges, used historically by mountaineering expeditions as a staging point before the final push toward Deo Tibba's technical climbing routes, and the site carries a slightly more serious, expedition-like atmosphere than the pastoral camps lower down. Marmots and occasional Himalayan blue sheep are sometimes spotted on the surrounding slopes, their presence a reminder of how this high pasture supports wildlife despite its stark appearance. The evening briefing here focuses on acclimatisation, since tomorrow's push toward Deo Tibba's base camp gains further altitude into terrain where mountain sickness symptoms become a real consideration, and guides monitor the group closely for any early signs of fatigue, headache, or appetite loss. Temperatures at Tainta drop sharply after dark, and most trekkers retreat to their tents earlier than on previous nights, conscious of the demanding day still ahead.
highlights: Moraine-edge trail tracing the Duhangan glacier's old retreat path
Expanding views of Deo Tibba's rock spires and ice fields
Expedition-style staging camp at Tainta
Possible blue sheep sightings on the surrounding slopes
tips: Watch closely for any altitude symptoms from this point onward and report them promptly
Boulder sections here are loose, place each step deliberately
Eat a full dinner even if appetite has started to dip with the altitude

deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.title: Tainta to Deo Tibba Base Camp
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.trailType: glacial moraine, boulder-strewn approach, high basin camp
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.description: The final approach to Deo Tibba's base camp moves entirely through high glacial terrain, the trail weaving across moraine ridges and boulder fields left by the mountain's retreating ice, with almost no vegetation remaining beyond the occasional patch of hardy lichen clinging to exposed rock. The gradient is not especially steep, but the terrain itself demands constant attention, loose rock and uneven footing making the pace deliberately slow and careful rather than fast. As the trail rounds a final moraine ridge, the base camp basin opens up in full, a wide, stony amphitheatre set directly beneath Deo Tibba's ice-clad northeast face, with the peak's summit ridge and hanging glaciers dominating the skyline in a way that feels genuinely close after days of watching it from a distance. This site has served mountaineering expeditions for decades, and remnants of old camps, from cairns to the occasional weathered rope anchor, hint at its long history as a staging ground for technical climbs on Deo Tibba and the nearby Indrasan massif. The basin's silence is profound, broken only by wind moving across bare rock and, occasionally, the distant, unsettling rumble of ice shifting somewhere on the glacier above, a sound that never fails to draw every head upward. Trekkers typically spend the afternoon exploring the immediate basin on foot, examining the glacier's blue-tinged crevasses from a safe distance and taking in views that stretch toward the Pir Panjal's outer ranges. Camp is pitched on the flattest available ground, and the evening briefing covers the trek's turning point, with tomorrow beginning the long descent back toward Jagatsukh. Cold here is intense and immediate once the sun drops behind the ridge, and most of the group retreats to sleeping bags well before full dark, tired but unmistakably satisfied at having reached one of the Kullu valley's most dramatic, seldom-visited high points.
highlights: Reaching Deo Tibba Base Camp beneath the mountain's ice-clad face
Remnants of historic mountaineering camps in the basin
Close views of glacier crevasses and hanging ice
Profound high-altitude silence broken only by distant glacial movement
tips: Keep a safe distance from the glacier's edge and crevasse zones at all times
This is the coldest camp of the trek, use every layer you have after dark
Take time to rest fully this evening, tomorrow begins a long two-day descent

deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.title: Deo Tibba Base Camp Descent to Seri
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.trailType: moraine descent, boulder field, returning alpine meadow
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.description: The descent from base camp begins with one last look back at Deo Tibba's face, now catching the low morning sun in sharp relief, before the trail drops steadily back through the moraine and boulder terrain climbed so carefully the day before. Moving downhill is naturally quicker, though the loose, uneven ground still demands real attention, and by late morning the first returning patches of alpine grass signal the gradual transition back toward gentler terrain. Passing back through Tainta, the pasture feels different in reverse, its expedition-camp atmosphere replaced by a more relaxed, familiar quality now that the trek's highest point lies safely behind the group. The Duhangan reappears as a livelier, more accessible stream lower down, and its cold water offers a welcome refreshment during the day's longer stretches of walking. Birch and rhododendron thicken again as the trail loses more height, and the return through this corridor feels notably different from the outbound climb, with afternoon light filtering through the canopy at a lower, warmer angle than the earlier morning ascent. Seri's wide grazing meadow, reached again by mid-to-late afternoon, offers the same restorative, open character it did on the way up, though now viewed with the particular satisfaction of a summit-basin objective already achieved rather than still ahead. Shepherd flocks are often still grazing here, their presence a comforting sign of returning to more populated, lower-altitude terrain after two days spent in the stark high basin near Deo Tibba. Dinner conversation naturally turns toward reflections on the base camp's dramatic scale and the eerie sound of shifting glacier ice, while the guide outlines the final, easier descent back to Jagatsukh planned for the following day.
highlights: Descending views of Deo Tibba's face in morning light
Return through Tainta's high pasture with an easier, settled feel
Rhododendron and birch forest revisited in afternoon light
Familiar shepherd flocks grazing at Seri meadow
tips: Knees take strain on long descents, use poles and shorten your stride on the steeper stretches
Refill water at the Duhangan crossings, sources become sparse again once past Seri tomorrow
Enjoy a slower pace today, the hardest technical ground of the trek is now behind you

deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.title: Seri to Jobri and Jagatsukh, Trek Ends in Manali
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.trailType: birch and deodar forest descent, terraced fields, village road
deo-tibba-base-camp-trek.description: The final day covers considerable ground, retracing the full descent from Seri through Jobri and down into Jagatsukh's terraced fields, and the change in scenery across the day feels almost like flipping back through the trek's earlier chapters in reverse. Birch forest gives way once more to deodar, the trail growing progressively softer and more shaded underfoot, and the sound of the Duhangan stays close throughout, now a familiar companion rather than a novel feature of the landscape. Local life becomes steadily more visible the lower the trail drops, with shepherds moving flocks toward lower pastures for the evening and the occasional mule train carrying supplies up toward higher grazing grounds, a reminder that this valley remains a working landscape rather than a purely recreational one. Reaching Jagatsukh again by early afternoon brings a genuine sense of completion, its ancient temples now viewed with fresh appreciation after six days spent tracing the valley's full length up to one of the Kullu region's most imposing peaks. A short drive from Jagatsukh returns the group to Manali, passing through Naggar's orchard belt and along the Beas river, with guides often using the journey to recap the trek's arc and answer any remaining questions about the glaciers, peaks, and terrain covered over the preceding days. Arriving back in Manali by evening closes out a route that, while demanding in both distance and cumulative altitude gain, rewards with a genuinely close, immersive look at one of the Kullu valley's grandest peaks, far removed from the more crowded trails found elsewhere in the region. For many in the group, the memory of standing in the silent, ice-rimmed basin beneath Deo Tibba's northeast face remains the trek's defining moment long after the walk itself has ended.
highlights: Full-length descent retracing the trek's earlier stages
Renewed appreciation for Jagatsukh's ancient temples
Scenic drive back to Manali through Naggar's orchard belt
Trek conclusion after six days of steady altitude progression
tips: Pace this long final day steadily, it is longer in distance than it may first appear
Keep your camera charged for the drive back, the Naggar stretch offers lovely valley views
Consider a short rest day in Manali afterward, given the trek's cumulative demands

chandrakhani-pass-trek.title: Naggar to Rumsu and On to Balu ka Bagh Camp
chandrakhani-pass-trek.trailType: deodar forest ridge, village lanes, open grazing shoulder
chandrakhani-pass-trek.description: This crossing of the Chandrakhani ridge begins in Naggar, where the group pauses briefly beneath the wooden and stone walls of the old Kullu capital's castle before heading out along a quiet lane toward Rumsu village. Rumsu's slate roofs and small shrine to Jamlu Devta mark the last proper settlement before the trail turns uphill in earnest, climbing through a deodar and horse-chestnut forest whose needle-strewn floor softens every footstep. The gradient builds steadily rather than sharply, and the group gains height through a series of long forest switchbacks, pausing at intervals to look back over the widening patchwork of Kullu's orchards and terraced barley fields far below. As the trees begin to thin, the trail crosses onto an open grazing shoulder used by shepherd families through the summer, and the views here expand considerably, taking in the snow-flecked line of the Pir Panjal to the north. Balu ka Bagh, the day's camp, occupies a broad grassy bench just below the treeline, chosen deliberately as a base for tomorrow's push over Chandrakhani rather than the lower forest camps used on some alternative routes into this region. From here, the pass itself is visible as a modest saddle on the ridge above, and guides use the clear afternoon light to point out the exact line the trail will follow. The evening turns cold quickly once the sun dips behind the ridge, and dinner is taken around a fire with detailed talk of the following day's crossing and Malana's famously self-governing culture on the far side. Unlike routes that exit the ridge toward Jari, this itinerary is built specifically as a there-and-back loop returning to Naggar, and the guide outlines that structure clearly this first evening.
highlights: Brief stop at Naggar Castle before the trailhead
Deodar forest switchbacks above Rumsu village
Open shepherd pastures with Pir Panjal views
Camp at Balu ka Bagh positioned for the pass crossing
tips: Take a few minutes at Naggar Castle before setting off, it is easy to skip in the rush to start
The forest switchbacks are shaded but still demand steady pacing, do not rush early on
Confirm with your guide that this is the loop-return itinerary rather than the Jari exit route

chandrakhani-pass-trek.title: Balu ka Bagh over Chandrakhani Pass to Malana
chandrakhani-pass-trek.trailType: open ridgeline, alpine scrub, steep scree descent into the Malana valley
chandrakhani-pass-trek.description: Today's climb leaves the last trees behind within the first half hour, the trail rising along a bare, wind-exposed ridge scattered with low juniper and cushions of alpine flowers that cling stubbornly to the rocky ground. As height builds, the panorama becomes genuinely vast, taking in the Deo Tibba and Indrasan massif to the northeast and the deep trench of the Parvati valley opening away to the south, a view that rewards every laboured step of the final approach. Chandrakhani Pass itself is marked by a weathered cairn strung with faded prayer flags, and on a clear morning the 360-degree outlook ranks among the finest short pass crossings in the Kullu region, taking in ridgelines folding away toward Manikaran and the more distant Pin Parvati range. The descent that follows demands real concentration, dropping through loose scree and narrow shepherd tracks into a steadily narrowing gorge where vegetation slowly returns, first as scrub, then birch, and finally the terraced fields that announce Malana below. The village appears abruptly, its timber-and-slate houses stacked steeply above the rushing Malana nala, and visitors are reminded firmly of local custom on arrival, avoiding contact with temple walls, shrines, or village structures out of respect for Malana's fiercely guarded self-governance under its own council, the Kanashi. A guesthouse room is arranged for the night, and the evening settles into quiet observation of a community that has, through geography and choice, remained distinct from the wider Kullu valley for generations, its own dialect and customs intact despite the modern hydel project humming in the background. Conversation over dinner naturally turns to what tomorrow's rest day in the village might reveal about this famously private, self-contained settlement.
highlights: Crossing Chandrakhani Pass at approximately 12,000 ft
Panoramic views of the Deo Tibba massif and Parvati valley
Steep, demanding descent into the isolated Malana gorge
Arrival in Malana's self-governing village culture
tips: Leave camp at first light, this is the longest and most exposed day of the trek
Observe Malana's no-touch customs around temples and homes without exception
Trekking poles help considerably on the loose scree descent into the village

chandrakhani-pass-trek.title: Rest and Culture Day in Malana Village
chandrakhani-pass-trek.trailType: village lanes, terraced field paths, riverside forest loop
chandrakhani-pass-trek.description: With the pass now behind the group, today is given over entirely to exploring Malana at an unhurried pace, wandering its terraced fields and narrow stone lanes while learning about a community that continues to function under its own parliamentary system rather than the panchayat structure used elsewhere in Himachal. A gentle walk down toward the Malana nala brings the group within respectful view of the Jamlu Devta temple, its wooden carvings among the oldest devotional structures in the wider Kullu region, though entry remains restricted to villagers alone in keeping with tradition. Guides typically share the local legend of Jamlu gathering the gods of the valley for a great feast, only for a bag of demons to be accidentally released when a curious deity peeked inside, an event many villagers credit for Malana's enduring separateness and distinctive Kanashi dialect, unrelated to neighbouring tongues. The remainder of the morning is spent observing daily village life, from wooden balconies stacked with drying maize to the steady rhythm of mules arriving with supplies, since no road reaches this settlement and everything from cement to rice travels in on someone's back. A short loop along the riverbank through birch and walnut trees offers a cooler alternative to the sun-warmed lanes, the Malana nala running fast and cold just below the path. As afternoon light softens, the high ridgelines visible from the village edge take on a warm, rose-gold cast, and the sheer isolation of the setting becomes especially apparent from this vantage point. Dinner is again simple, usually dal, rice, and a mountain green cooked in local ghee, and the guide uses the evening to prepare the group for tomorrow's return crossing of Chandrakhani back toward Naggar, closing the loop rather than continuing onward.
highlights: Exploring Malana's terraced fields and stacked stone lanes
Respectful distant view of the restricted Jamlu Devta temple
Learning about Malana's unique self-governing council, the Kanashi
Quiet riverside forest loop along the Malana nala
tips: Do not attempt to photograph the temple interior or enter restricted areas
Ask before photographing villagers, as some prefer not to be filmed
Use today to rest properly, tomorrow's return crossing repeats yesterday's demanding climb

chandrakhani-pass-trek.title: Malana Back over Chandrakhani Pass to Naggar
chandrakhani-pass-trek.trailType: steep scree climb, open ridgeline, forested descent to the roadhead
chandrakhani-pass-trek.description: The final day closes the loop by climbing back out of Malana's gorge the same way the group descended two days earlier, though tackling this steep scree slope uphill proves a noticeably different challenge than the earlier descent. Progress is slow and deliberate through the lower gorge, with the trail gaining height quickly through returning scrub and birch before opening once more onto Chandrakhani's bare, windswept ridge. Reaching the pass a second time offers a chance to properly linger, weather permitting, over views that were necessarily rushed on the first, longer crossing day, and the familiar cairn and faded prayer flags feel almost like old acquaintances by now. From the pass, the descent toward Naggar follows the outbound route back through Balu ka Bagh's grazing shoulder and into the deodar forest switchbacks climbed on day one, the downhill gradient making for considerably faster progress despite the day's overall length. Passing back through Rumsu in the afternoon, the village feels different in reverse, its slate roofs and Jamlu Devta shrine now viewed with the particular satisfaction of a completed loop rather than an outbound curiosity. The final stretch into Naggar retraces quiet lanes past orchard plots, and the old castle reappears on its promontory above the Beas river as the trek's natural closing landmark. Arriving back where the journey began, rather than exiting via an unfamiliar trailhead, gives this route its distinct character among Chandrakhani crossings, and the group typically gathers for a final tea in Naggar before onward transport to Manali. Reflecting on four days that combined a genuine high pass, a rare glimpse into Malana's self-contained culture, and a satisfying return to the exact point of departure, most trekkers agree the loop structure adds a sense of completeness rarely found on one-way pass crossings elsewhere in the region.
highlights: Second, more relaxed crossing of Chandrakhani Pass
Completing a full loop back to the Naggar trailhead
Revisiting Rumsu village from the opposite direction
Trek conclusion beneath Naggar Castle
tips: This is the longest day of the trek by distance, start as early as possible
Your legs will feel yesterday's climb, take the ridge descent to Balu ka Bagh steadily
Arrange onward transport from Naggar to Manali in advance, as options thin out by evening

sar-pass-trek.title: Kasol to Grahan Village
sar-pass-trek.trailType: riverside forest trail, cantilever bridge crossing, terraced village approach
sar-pass-trek.description: The Sar Pass trek sets off from Kasol, the small riverside town that has become the unofficial gateway to the Parvati valley's trekking routes, its cafes and backpacker lanes giving way quickly to quieter forest once the trail proper begins. Crossing the Parvati river on a cantilevered wooden bridge, the route enters a shaded corridor of pine and walnut, climbing steadily alongside a tributary stream whose water runs cold and clear even in peak summer. The gradient builds in stages rather than one sustained push, with periodic flatter stretches offering a chance to catch breath and take in the widening view back down toward Kasol's rooftops, increasingly small against the valley floor. Wild marijuana grows informally along parts of this route, a long-standing feature of the region's flora though commercial trade in it remains illegal and best avoided entirely by visitors. As the trail climbs higher, walnut and pine give way to oak, and the first terraced fields belonging to Grahan come into view, cut into a steep hillside in a pattern that has changed little in generations. Grahan itself is a close-knit village of wooden, slate-roofed houses, considerably less visited than Kasol despite being only a day's walk away, and its guesthouses offer simple but genuinely warm hospitality along with home-cooked meals that make full use of locally grown vegetables and grains. An early evening walk through the village's narrow lanes reveals small temples and drying racks of maize, while views back down the valley toward Kasol provide a satisfying sense of the day's altitude gain. Guides use the evening to introduce the trek's overall arc, from Grahan's forested approach to the high, snow-streaked pass still four days ahead, and dinner is eaten with the particular appetite that comes from a genuinely full first day on the trail.
highlights: Cantilever bridge crossing over the Parvati river
Steady forest climb through pine, walnut, and oak
Terraced fields and traditional architecture in Grahan village
Home-cooked meals and genuine hospitality away from Kasol's bustle
tips: Start early to beat the midday heat on the exposed lower switchbacks
Do not engage with informally grown cannabis along the trail, it remains illegal to trade
Sample the local Himachali thali if offered in Grahan, portions are generous and filling

sar-pass-trek.title: Grahan to Min Thach
sar-pass-trek.trailType: dense forest ascent, waterfall crossing, alpine meadow camp
sar-pass-trek.description: Leaving Grahan's terraced fields behind, the trail plunges back into forest, this time a denser mix of oak and rhododendron that closes overhead and keeps the climb pleasantly shaded through the morning's steepest stretches. A waterfall crossing partway up the route requires careful footing on wet rock, the spray from the falling water a cool, welcome distraction from the sustained gradient, and several trekkers pause here longer than strictly necessary simply to enjoy the spectacle. Beyond the falls, the forest thins gradually, oak giving way to birch and finally to open meadow as the trail breaks free of the canopy entirely in the day's final hour. Min Thach, the camp for the night, occupies a broad, gently sloping meadow ringed by forest on one side and opening toward higher, increasingly bare ridgelines on the other, a clear signal that the trek's more alpine stretches begin here in earnest. Wildflowers, including clusters of primula and anemone, dot the grass through the summer season, and the site's relative shelter from wind makes for a comfortable overnight despite the meaningful altitude gained over the day. Views from camp stretch back down toward the forested slopes climbed since morning, while ahead, glimpses of the higher ridge toward Nagaru hint at the terrain still to come. Guides use the evening to talk through the trek's escalating demands, noting that tomorrow's walk to Nagaru pushes well above the treeline for the first time, and to remind the group of pacing strategies suited to the thinning air. Campfire conversation tends to circle around the waterfall crossing and the dramatic transition from forest to open meadow experienced within a single day's walk.
highlights: Waterfall crossing on the climb out of Grahan
Transition from oak and rhododendron forest to open meadow
Wildflower-covered camp at Min Thach
First clear views toward the higher Nagaru ridge
tips: Take extra care crossing wet rock near the waterfall, footing is deceptively slick
Layer up for the evening, Min Thach's open meadow loses heat quickly after sunset
Pace today steadily, tomorrow's walk climbs well above the treeline

sar-pass-trek.title: Min Thach to Nagaru
sar-pass-trek.trailType: open ridge meadow, exposed alpine slope, high camp on a narrow shoulder
sar-pass-trek.description: Today's walk is short in distance but significant in character, climbing entirely above the treeline onto an open, increasingly narrow ridge shoulder that offers some of the widest, most uninterrupted views on the entire Sar Pass route. The trail rises steadily through alpine grass and scattered rock, the thinning air making the moderate gradient feel more demanding than its modest length would suggest, and short rest stops become a natural part of the rhythm. As height builds, the panorama expands to take in a sweep of peaks across the Parvati valley, with the snow-streaked line of the pass itself, still a day away, becoming visible for the first time on the horizon ahead. Nagaru occupies a narrow, wind-exposed shoulder perched dramatically above the surrounding valleys, and the site is chosen specifically for this vantage rather than for shelter, meaning tents are pitched with extra care and secured firmly against gusts that can build quickly in the late afternoon. Sunset from Nagaru is widely regarded as one of the finest on this trek, the surrounding peaks catching successive layers of orange, pink, and finally deep blue as darkness falls, and most groups gather outside their tents specifically to watch the light show despite the cold. This high camp also marks the psychological turning point of the trek, with tomorrow's push over Sar Pass itself standing as the clear centrepiece of the entire route, and guides use the evening briefing to walk through timing, layering, and pacing in considerable detail. Sleep at this altitude often feels lighter than usual, a normal response the guide monitors, and most trekkers turn in early, aware of the demanding, early start required for the following day's crossing.
highlights: Fully open, above-treeline ridge walk with sweeping valley views
First direct sighting of Sar Pass on the horizon
Dramatic, wind-exposed high camp at Nagaru
One of the trek's most memorable sunsets over the surrounding peaks
tips: Secure your tent guy-lines firmly, gusts at Nagaru can be sudden and strong
Watch the sunset from outside your tent if weather allows, it is genuinely worth the cold
Sleep in every layer you are comfortable in tonight, temperatures fall well below freezing

sar-pass-trek.title: Nagaru over Sar Pass to Biskeri Thach
sar-pass-trek.trailType: snow-covered ridge approach, high pass crossing, long meadow descent
sar-pass-trek.description: This is the day the entire trek builds toward, and the group leaves Nagaru in cold pre-dawn light to take advantage of firm snow before the sun softens the upper slopes later in the morning. The climb toward Sar Pass moves over increasingly snow-covered ground for much of the year, and trekkers typically use gaiters and, when conditions call for it, basic crampons or careful step-kicking to manage the firmer patches, the thinning air making every stride feel more deliberate than lower down. Reaching the top of Sar Pass at nearly 14,000 feet rewards the effort with a genuinely vast panorama, the Parvati valley receding behind in a wash of green and grey while ahead, the terrain toward Biskeri and the Kasol side unfolds in gentler, forested folds. Prayer flags and a simple cairn mark the highest point, and most groups pause here for photographs and a shared sense of achievement before beginning what is, for many, the trek's single most enjoyable stretch: a long, exhilarating slide or glissade down a sustained snow slope on the pass's far side, weather and season permitting. Below the snow, the trail continues down through increasingly grassy terrain, the gradient easing gradually as birch and then pine reappear on the lower slopes. Biskeri Thach, the night's camp, sits in a broad meadow well below the pass, its relative warmth and shelter a welcome contrast to the previous night's exposed ridge, and reaching it after such a long, demanding day brings real relief to tired legs. Dinner conversation is dominated, unsurprisingly, by replays of the pass crossing and the snow slide, each trekker recounting their own version of the day's undisputed highlight.
highlights: Crossing Sar Pass at nearly 14,000 ft
Optional snow slide descent on the pass's far side
Dramatic shift from snow-bound ridge to forested meadow terrain
Sheltered, restorative camp at Biskeri Thach
tips: Gaiters are essential if snow cover is significant, they keep boots dry through the crossing
Follow your guide's exact line on the snow slope, hidden rocks can lurk just beneath the surface
Celebrate the crossing, but keep some energy in reserve, the descent to Biskeri is still long

sar-pass-trek.title: Biskeri Thach to Barshaini, Trek Ends
sar-pass-trek.trailType: forested ridge descent, village approach trail, motorable roadhead
sar-pass-trek.description: The final day continues the descent begun the previous afternoon, dropping steadily through thickening pine and deodar forest as the trail loses the last of the trek's significant altitude on its way toward Barshaini. The gradient is manageable rather than brutal, though legs already tired from the previous day's pass crossing are tested again on the long series of switchbacks, and the forest's cooler shade offers welcome relief as the morning sun strengthens. Views open intermittently through gaps in the canopy toward the Parvati valley's lower reaches, distinctly greener and more settled in character than the stark high terrain crossed over the previous two days, and the increasing sound of vehicle traffic drifts up from the valley floor well before Barshaini itself comes into view. Small hamlets and terraced fields appear with growing frequency as the trail descends, a clear sign of returning to the valley's more populated stretch after days spent largely in wilderness. Barshaini, a small village and roadhead known also as the starting point for the nearby Kheerganga trail, marks the formal end of the Sar Pass crossing, its handful of dhabas and guesthouses offering a welcome final meal before the drive back toward Kasol. Guides typically use the descent's final hour to recap the trek's full arc, from Grahan's terraced fields through Nagaru's exposed high camp to the unforgettable morning at Sar Pass itself, and to answer any lingering questions about peaks and features seen along the way. The short drive from Barshaini back into Kasol closes out a route widely regarded as one of Himachal's most rewarding introductions to genuine high-altitude trekking, combining forest, meadow, snow, and a properly earned mountain pass within a single accessible week.
highlights: Long forested descent through pine and deodar
Return to settled terraced farmland near Barshaini
Trek conclusion at the Barshaini roadhead
Recap drive back into Kasol through the lower Parvati valley
tips: Use poles on the extended descent to protect your knees after a demanding pass day
Keep some cash handy for a final meal at Barshaini's dhabas
Confirm your Kasol transfer timing with the operator before the descent begins

kheerganga-trek.title: Barshaini to Kheerganga
kheerganga-trek.trailType: riverside forest trail, waterfall crossing, pine and deodar ascent
kheerganga-trek.description: The walk to Kheerganga begins at Barshaini, where the trail drops briefly to cross the Parvati river before settling into a long, steady climb through forest that keeps the route shaded for much of the day. Rudra Nag waterfall, a dramatic cascade roughly midway along the route, offers a natural rest point, its spray cooling the air noticeably and drawing most trekkers to linger a few extra minutes on the rocks nearby. Beyond the falls, the trail continues through pine and deodar, the gradient building in manageable stages rather than one sustained push, and small dhabas run by local families appear at intervals, offering tea, Maggi noodles, and a chance to rest legs already feeling the accumulated climb. The forest opens gradually in the trail's final stretch, and the first hint of Kheerganga's famous plateau appears as scattered structures and prayer flags come into view against a backdrop of surrounding peaks. Kheerganga itself holds deep religious significance, believed in local tradition to be the spot where Lord Shiva meditated for thousands of years, and a small temple and cluster of guesthouses and camps now occupy the open meadow that gives the site its name, loosely translating to a place of milk, in reference to the color of its hot spring water. The undoubted highlight awaiting weary trekkers is the natural sulphur hot spring at the plateau's edge, its steaming pools offering genuine, well-earned relief after a full day's climb, and most of the group makes straight for the water within minutes of arriving. Sunset from the plateau, with peaks turning shades of pink and gold across the valley, draws almost everyone back outside after the soak, and the evening settles into the relaxed, sociable atmosphere for which Kheerganga has become known among Parvati valley trekkers.
highlights: Rudra Nag waterfall as a scenic midway rest point
Steady forested climb with local dhabas along the route
Natural hot sulphur springs at the Kheerganga plateau
Sunset views over the surrounding Parvati valley peaks
tips: Carry a swimsuit or quick-dry clothing specifically for the hot spring
Break the climb into stages using the trailside dhabas rather than rushing straight through
Arrive with enough daylight left to enjoy the springs before the evening chill sets in

kheerganga-trek.title: Kheerganga Descent to Barshaini
kheerganga-trek.trailType: forested descent, riverside trail, waterfall crossing
kheerganga-trek.description: Most trekkers wake early on the second morning specifically to catch the plateau in its quietest, most atmospheric state, mist often lingering over the meadow before the sun clears the surrounding ridgelines, and a final soak in the hot springs before breakfast has become something of an unofficial ritual for those completing this short but memorable trek. The descent back toward Barshaini retraces the previous day's route, and moving downhill through the same pine and deodar forest feels noticeably quicker, though knees register the cumulative effect of the steady loss in elevation over the following hours. The trailside dhabas that offered rest stops on the way up serve the same purpose in reverse, and a final cup of tea at one of these simple wooden shacks makes for an easy, unhurried pace back down. Rudra Nag's waterfall, passed again roughly midway through the descent, looks different in the softer light of morning, and many trekkers pause here a second time simply to enjoy the spray before continuing on. As the trail loses more height, signs of the wider Parvati valley reappear, terraced fields and the increasing sound of the river signalling the approaching end of the walk. Reaching Barshaini by early afternoon brings the short trek to its natural close, and the ease of access, being just a short drive from Kasol, makes Kheerganga a genuinely popular choice for those seeking a proper taste of Himalayan forest, hot springs, and mountain culture without committing to a longer expedition. Many extend their day with a stop in Kasol itself before heading onward, carrying with them the particular, relaxed satisfaction that only a hot spring soak at altitude seems able to provide.
highlights: Early morning mist and a final soak at the hot springs
Quicker, easier descent through familiar forest terrain
A second, different-light view of Rudra Nag waterfall
Trek conclusion with easy onward access to Kasol
tips: Wake early for a quieter, mist-filled experience of the plateau before departure
Use trekking poles on the descent, the cumulative downhill strain adds up quickly
Keep a change of dry clothes ready for after your final hot spring soak

pin-parvati-pass-trek.title: Kasol to Barshaini and On to Kheerganga
pin-parvati-pass-trek.trailType: riverside road walk, forested ascent, waterfall crossing
pin-parvati-pass-trek.description: The classic Pin Parvati crossing begins gently, with a short drive or walk from Kasol along the Parvati river to Barshaini, where the trail proper turns uphill through forest that will remain the group's companion for most of the day. Rudra Nag waterfall marks a natural midpoint, its cool spray a welcome pause on a climb that, while not especially steep, gains real cumulative height by the time Kheerganga's open plateau finally appears. Reaching Kheerganga on the very first day carries particular weight on this eleven-day route, since the plateau's natural hot springs, sacred in local tradition as the meditation site of Lord Shiva, represent the last significant comfort before the trail turns decisively wild over the days ahead. Most of the group makes directly for the steaming pools on arrival, easing legs that will need every ounce of recovery available before the sustained, remote stretches beyond. Guides use the relatively easy first day to run through the trek's full arc in detail, from the succession of increasingly remote camps at Tunda Bhuj, Thakur Kuan, and Odi Thach to the technical crossing of Pin Parvati Pass itself and the eventual descent into Spiti's stark Pin Valley. This is deliberately framed as a serious, committing expedition rather than a standard trek, given the total absence of any exit points once the route moves beyond Odi Thach, and the evening briefing covers everything from expected weather patterns to the self-sufficient nature of the days ahead. Sunset over the plateau, watched from beside the hot springs as peaks turn shades of gold and rose, offers a suitably memorable start to what will prove one of Himachal's longest and most rewarding classic treks.
highlights: Rudra Nag waterfall en route to Kheerganga
Final hot spring soak before the trek's wilder stretches begin
Detailed briefing on the full eleven-day route ahead
Sunset over the sacred Kheerganga plateau
tips: Soak properly at Kheerganga tonight, it is the last hot spring access for the rest of the trek
Listen closely to the full-route briefing, this is a serious, committing expedition beyond day five
Pack efficiently from the start, supply points beyond Kheerganga are effectively nonexistent

pin-parvati-pass-trek.title: Kheerganga to Tunda Bhuj
pin-parvati-pass-trek.trailType: birch forest trail, riverside meadow, narrowing valley path
pin-parvati-pass-trek.description: Leaving Kheerganga's warm springs behind, the trail settles into a quieter, wilder rhythm as it follows the upper Parvati river deeper into a narrowing valley flanked by birch forest and increasingly rugged rock walls. The path undulates rather than climbing hard today, tracing the river's contour through meadows that see far fewer visitors than the well-trodden ground below Kheerganga, and the sense of moving into genuinely remote terrain builds steadily with every passing hour. Cannabis grows wild in scattered patches along this stretch, part of the region's long-standing ecology though never a focus for the trek itself, and guides typically note it in passing before moving attention back to the river, peaks, and increasingly cold, glacier-fed water rushing alongside the trail.
